did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a skin cancer diagnosis application using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model trained on the HAM10000 dataset to classify skin lesions (Melanocytic skin Nevus, Melanoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Pyogenic Granuloma, and Actinic Keratosis). The study reports experimental results, including an accuracy of approximately 75% on the test set and up to 0.78 validation accuracy with additional MLP layers, demonstrating the use of deep learning for dermatological classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design and implementation of a full-stack skin cancer diagnosis application using a CNN model trained on the HAM10000 dataset. It details the methodology (training process, service hosting, NLP integration), reports specific experimental results (accuracy of ~75% on test set, 0.78 validation accuracy), and includes a conclusion and references, which are characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Full-Stack Application of Skin Cancer Diagnosis Based on CNN Model
journal: 2021 IEEE International Conference on Computer Science, Electronic Information Engineering and Intelligent Control Technology (CEI)
brief_summary: This study developed a full-stack iOS skin cancer diagnosis application utilizing a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model trained on the HAM10000 dataset to classify six types of skin lesions. The system integrates a Keras-based CNN with categorical and numerical Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) layers to incorporate user metadata such as age and gender. The application features a Swift UI frontend, an ExpressJS backend, and an integrated Natural Language Processing chatbot for user consultation. Model performance was evaluated using loss and accuracy metrics on a dataset split into 8000 training and 2016 validation entries. The experimental results demonstrated that adding numeric MLP features significantly improved performance, achieving a validation accuracy of 0.78. The final application successfully operates on the iOS environment, providing users with self-diagnosis capabilities and interactive support.

all_task_types.target: Six discrete skin lesion classes (Melanocytic skin Nevus, Melanoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Pyogenic Granuloma, and Actinic Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a CNN model trained on the HAM10000 dataset to classify skin lesions into six specific diagnostic categories. The model outputs one-hot encoded labels for these classes. Although the text mentions 'object detection' in the context of handling user input noise and transferring the classification model, the actual training and evaluation (using loss and accuracy metrics on class labels) are performed for multi-class classification. The final application predicts a single diagnostic category for the input image.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, used as the primary dataset for training the CNN model. The paper also mentions using 'more image data of a healthy person’s arm as default data set' to address differences between the dataset and actual user inputs, though specific details on this additional data are not provided.

use.training: Used to train three variations of the model: a CNN classification model, a CNN with categorical MLP, and a CNN with categorical and numerical MLP. The dataset was randomized and split into the first 8000 entries for training and the rest for validation.
use.validation: Used to evaluate model performance during training, achieving a validation accuracy of 0.78 for the model with categorical and numerical MLP.
use.testing: The abstract mentions an accuracy of around 75% for the 'HAM10000 test set', but the methodology section only describes a training/validation split. No specific test set counts or separate testing procedure details are provided in the experimental section.
use.other: The paper notes that the model was initially trained on HAM10000 but then transferred to an image detection model to handle user input noise. Additionally, the study mentions using 'more image data of a healthy person’s arm' to account for differences between the dataset's front captures and actual user inputs, but does not provide counts or details for this additional data.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using loss and accuracy metrics on a training/validation split, with qualitative comparisons between model architectures.

CNN classification model.value: Not explicitly reported as a final number, but described as the baseline with lower performance compared to MLP-integrated models.

CNN with categorical MLP.value: Improved over baseline CNN, but specific value not reported.

HAM10000.notes: The paper reports an accuracy of around 75% for the HAM10000 test set in the abstract, but the experimental section details a training/validation split (8000/2016) where the CNN with categorical and numerical MLP achieved a validation accuracy of 0.78. The addition of numeric MLP (age) provided the most significant improvement. Overfitting was noted not to occur.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) integration

additional_components: Categorical MLP (for gender, place, dx type)
architecture_details.integration_method: Concatenate layer combining CNN, categorical MLP, and numerical MLP outputs

training_details.loss_function: Binary Cross-Entropy (implied by loss formula in Eq. 1)
training_details.random_seed: Used to ensure consistent dataset shuffling across models

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: CNN with categorical and numerical MLP
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images from 600x450 pixels to 300x225 pixels
Normalization of three-color channels for image input
One-hot encoding for categorical features (gender, place, dx type)
conclusions: The study successfully developed a full-stack iOS skin cancer diagnosis application that integrates a CNN model with a Natural Language Processing (NLP) chatbot for user consultation.
The CNN model, trained on the HAM10000 dataset, achieved an accuracy of approximately 75% on the test set.
Integrating categorical and numerical Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) layers with the CNN model significantly improved performance, achieving a validation accuracy of 0.78.
The addition of the numerical MLP (specifically the age variable) provided the most significant improvement in model accuracy compared to the categorical MLP.
The experimental results indicated that overfitting did not occur during the training process.
The application is currently limited to the iOS platform; future work includes developing versions for Android and Harmony OS.
Future improvements involve enhancing the feedback function by cooperating with hospitals and clinics to synchronize patient reports for continuous model improvement.
important_remarks: The reported accuracy of ~75% is for the HAM10000 test set, but the methodology only describes an 8000/2016 training/validation split; the existence and composition of a separate test set are not explicitly detailed in the experimental section.

The study acknowledges a significant domain shift between the HAM10000 dataset (front captures of diseased areas) and actual user inputs (noisy, non-frontal images), but does not provide quantitative evidence that the proposed image detection model successfully mitigates this bias compared to the classification model.
The model performance (validation accuracy 0.78) is moderate for a multi-class skin cancer classification task, which may limit its clinical reliability as a standalone diagnostic tool.
The application is currently limited to the iOS platform, restricting its generalizability and deployment reach.
The paper relies on a feedback loop with hospitals/clinics for future improvement, but no data or results from such external validation or clinical integration are presented in this work.

The loss function formula provided (Eq. 1) corresponds to Binary Cross-Entropy, which is technically inconsistent with the multi-class classification task described (which typically requires Categorical Cross-Entropy), suggesting a potential methodological reporting error.
